text: Spleen fractures are traumatic injuries which most frequently result from a close trauma following a direct shock in the left thoraco-abdominal region. One should be most wary of spleen fractures when, in addition to the shock, there is a muscle guard of the left hypochondrium, a clinical suspicion of rib fracture to either ribs nine, ten, or eleven, the presence of a care sign on the left side that is an ascending scapular pain, signs of hemorrhagic shock, drop in blood pressure, increase of heart rate, metabolic acidosis. Hemoperitoneum must be actively looked for by echography. Focused assessment sonography for trauma, FAST for short, is designed to achieve this goal.

The spleen is an abdominal lymphoid organ located in the left hypochondrium. It may be concerned by every thoraco-abdominal trauma, especially those involving the ninth, tenth, and eleventh ribs. Its functions are the introduction of antigens and the filtering of aging blood cells. It is in contact with other organs, to the front, the posterior side of the stomach, by the gastrolenal ligament, and with the coda pancreatis via the splenopancreatic ligament, above the left diaphragm. behind the upper pole of the left kidney, and it is linked to the left colon flexure by the splenocolic ligament. The splenic vascular anatomy includes the splenic artery, the short gastric vessels which begin at the gastro-mental artery, or which are terminal ramifications of the splenic artery, the inferior and superior polar arteries, the splenic vein on the posterior aspect of the pancreas.

Sterile drapes are placed to expose the trunk from the sternal manubrium to the pubic symphysis. The surgeon performs an exifopubic median laparotomy, providing comprehensive access to the abdominal cavity for lesion control.

Before surgery, the surgical team has checked the necessary equipment and instruments which are, one, ideally two air pulsated heating blankets, the first is placed over the head and the upper limbs and the other over the lower limbs, a scalpel, retractors, two aortic clamps for the splenic hilum, blood products and a cell saver, two surgical suctions and forty swabs.

The surgeon eviscerates the small bowel to rapidly expose the abdominal cavity, using a reniform bowl to scoop out the haemoperitoneum for treatment and reinfusion. The abdominal cavity is then packed, starting with the most critical bleeder and continuing region by region in a clockwise manner, including the left hypochondrium, right hypochondrium, left lumbar, left iliac fossa, pelvis, right iliac fossa, right lumbar, and umbilical regions.

The surgeon places primary packing in the abdominal cavity to assess injuries and temporarily control hemorrhage. Removal of the natural abdominal swab via laparotomy and mobilization of the viscera reactivate hemorrhage, worsening the shock, while the anesthetic team works to stabilize the patient.

The surgeon removes the packing from the abdominal cavity, starting with the region assessed to be the least damaged, to clear the workspace for addressing the critical region in the left hypochondrium.

The surgeon exposes the spleen and carefully clamps the splenic hilum to avoid damaging the caudopancreatic region. The splenocolic ligament is divided, providing access to the posterior side of the spleen.

The surgeon clamps the short vessels around the spleen, ligates them, and divides them in stages. Following this, the splenic hilum is ligated and sectioned, completing the separation of the spleen from its vascular attachments.

The surgeon examines the complete abdominal cavity, including the omental bursa, systematically inspecting the small bowel, colon, retroperitoneum, and diaphragm for any lesions. The hemostasis is checked to ensure it remains under control.

A Radon drain is placed in the left hypochondrium near the caudal pancreas to manage potential iatrogenic fistulas. The surgeon thoroughly cleanses the splenic compartment with physiological serum heated to forty degrees Celsius, aiming to prevent hypothermia and contamination.

The latter can be responsible for inflammation and secondary tissue lesions. Depending on the stability of the patient, the abdomen is either stitched or left open. Splenectomy is an emergency procedure. It must be performed with both speed and respect for the principles that have been described during this presentation. Did you get it?
